moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unconventional first move that immediately contests the center and prepares to support d4 or e4. The Van Geet Opening (1.Nc3) is a flexible system that avoids the heavily analyzed main lines—instead of committing our pawn structure immediately, we develop our knight to a natural square while keeping multiple strategic options open. This move controls key central squares like d5 and e4, and allows us to adapt our setup based on Black's response, making it popular at all levels for players who enjoy dynamic, off-beat positions.

Black chooses a fianchetto setup, preparing to develop the bishop to g7 where it will control the long diagonal toward a1. This move signals Black's intent to play a flexible, hypermodern position—Black isn't rushing to occupy the center with pawns but will instead control it from a distance. The move also prepares to potentially support an f5 advance or create a solid kingside structure.

We immediately launch the Myers Attack by pushing the h-pawn, creating immediate threats on the kingside while Black's pieces are undeveloped. This move prevents Black's fianchetto bishop from being comfortably placed on g7 (since we threaten h5 to disrupt it) and prepares to open lines on the kingside by force. By playing this aggressively before developing our kingside pieces, we seize the initiative and put pressure on Black to respond precisely—we're betting on creating concrete threats faster than Black can consolidate their setup.
